sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 11.64 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.11)

statement.latex: Let$(G)$denote the set of prime divisors of the order of a finite group~$G$. Are there only finitely many finite simple groups$G$, different from alternating groups, which have a proper subgroup$H$such that$(H)= (G)$? { V.\,S.\,Monakhov} No, there are infinitely many such groups. If$G=S_{4k}(2^s)$and$H ^{-}_{4k}(2^s)$, then$(G)= (H)$(V.\,I.\,Zenkov, Letter of March, 10, 1994.)

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory I_INFINITE_SEARCH; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
